column2: A facility must stop vehicles from approaching an entrance while still directing people toward a pedestrian checkpoint. Which physical control fits?
column5: Bollards are vehicle-resistant barriers. They manage physical approach to an area; they do not authenticate the people who pass between them.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Bollards Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which physical control uses two interlocked doors so one door remains secured while passage through the other is controlled?
column5: The two-door design limits the flow of people through a checkpoint. A fence or bollard directs movement but does not provide this controlled intermediate space.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield PhysicalSecurity AccessControl Source::Messer-v140

column2: What is the purpose of a badge reader at a controlled entrance?
column4: It reads an identification credential so the access system can allow or deny entry.
column5: The credential may use RFID, NFC, or a magnetic stripe. Reading a badge is an automated access check; a printed badge viewed by a guard is a manual check.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Badges Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which physical security measure provides recorded visual evidence for live monitoring or later review?
column5: Surveillance is primarily a detection and evidence control. It does not by itself prevent entry, so facilities often pair it with barriers or access controls.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Surveillance Source::Messer-v140

column2: How does a motion sensor differ from an alarm system in a physical-security design?
column4: A motion sensor detects movement; an alarm system signals that a configured security event has occurred.
column5: A motion sensor can feed an alarm system, but the detector and the notification system are different roles. Alarms may also react to doors, windows, or duress inputs.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Detection Source::Messer-v140

column2: What security boundary is a door lock primarily intended to enforce?
column4: Entry into a room, building, or other enclosed area.
column5: Door locks may be mechanical, electronic, token-based, biometric, or multifactor. Their shared purpose is controlling passage through the doorway.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Locks Source::Messer-v140

column2: A shared data-center room is already access controlled, but one team must also restrict physical access to its own servers. Which control addresses that need?
column4: Equipment locks, such as a locked rack or cabinet.
column5: The room lock protects the area. An equipment lock adds a narrower boundary around a particular asset or group of assets.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Equipment Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which physical control can verify an employee's identity, enforce a visitor access list, and respond to unusual activity at an entrance?
column5: A guard can interpret circumstances and manage exceptions that an automated reader cannot. Consistent procedures and visitor records make that judgment auditable.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Guards Source::Messer-v140

column2: What is the primary security purpose of a fence around a facility?
column4: To establish and reinforce a physical perimeter.
column5: Fence height, visibility, cut resistance, and anti-climb features affect its strength, but the stable concept is the perimeter boundary.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 24
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Perimeter Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which small carried token commonly unlocks a door through proximity or contactless operation instead of a mechanical key?
column5: A key fob is something the user possesses. Its convenience does not prove that the holder is the authorized person unless another factor is also required.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalAccess KeyFob Source::Messer-v140

column2: An organization wants a carried card that stores a certificate-based credential and works with a reader. Which access technology fits?
column5: The smart card supplies a possession factor. Requiring a PIN or biometric with it adds a different factor rather than merely another possession item.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalAccess SmartCard Source::Messer-v140

column2: What is the purpose of a mobile digital key?
column4: It lets an authorized mobile device act as a key for a compatible lock.
column5: The phone becomes the carried credential, commonly after the user unlocks or otherwise verifies access to the device.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalAccess MobileDevice Source::Messer-v140

column2: Why should shared mechanical keys be stored in a controlled key cabinet with a check-in/check-out record?
column4: To control who can obtain each key and create an accountability trail for its use.
column5: A mechanical key may be necessary for a standalone lock, but a loose key has no built-in identity or event log. The management process supplies that missing control.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalAccess Keys Accountability Source::Messer-v140

column2: Why does compromise of biometric authentication data create a different long-term risk than compromise of a password?
column4: A password can be replaced, but a person's biometric trait is difficult or impossible to change.
column5: Biometric systems generally compare a stored mathematical representation of a trait. Protecting that representation matters because the underlying fingerprint, face, retina, palm, or voice belongs permanently to the user.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield PhysicalAccess Biometrics Risk Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which biometric scanner identifies a person from the distinctive capillary pattern at the back of the eye?
column5: Retina scanning uses an internal eye pattern. It should not be confused with facial recognition, which builds a representation from facial features.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalAccess Biometrics Retina Source::Messer-v140

column2: What physical characteristic does facial recognition technology use as an authentication key?
column4: A digital representation of the user's facial features.
column5: Facial recognition is a biometric factor. Camera and depth-sensing techniques vary, so the durable exam concept is the trait being evaluated.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalAccess Biometrics FRT Source::Messer-v140

column2: How does voice recognition technology authenticate a user?
column4: It compares the user's spoken voice with a stored voice representation.
column5: The system evaluates who is speaking, not merely whether the words are correct. Environmental noise or inability to speak can make this factor impractical alone.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalAccess Biometrics Voice Source::Messer-v140

column2: How can improved lighting strengthen physical security around an entrance?
column4: It increases visibility for people and cameras and reduces concealment.
column5: Lighting is both a deterrence and visibility control. Placement matters because glare or deep shadows can reduce rather than improve useful visibility.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Lighting Deterrence Source::Messer-v140

column2: A checkpoint must screen people and bags for metal objects. Which control is appropriate, and what important limitation does it have?
column4: A magnetometer; it does not detect nonmetallic objects.
column5: A magnetometer is a screening detector, not an authentication factor. It says nothing about whether the person carrying an object is authorized.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 25
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic PhysicalSecurity Magnetometer Screening Source::Messer-v140

column2: A user's account can perform every assigned job task but cannot install unapproved software or administer other users. Which principle is being applied?
column5: Least privilege minimizes permissions to the job requirement. It is an authorization principle, not a method of proving the user's identity.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 26
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield LogicalSecurity LeastPrivilege Authorization Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which security model rejects the assumption that a user, device, or process is trustworthy merely because it is already inside the network?
column5: Zero Trust applies verification and policy across users, devices, and processes. Least privilege narrows granted rights; Zero Trust is the broader approach that avoids implicit trust.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 26
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield LogicalSecurity ZeroTrust Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which logical control uses permit or deny rules to decide whether matching network traffic or resource access is allowed?
column5: Network ACL criteria can include source, destination, protocol, and port. The same allow/deny concept also appears in operating-system resource permissions.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 26
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield LogicalSecurity ACL NetworkSecurity Source::Messer-v140

column2: Why do a password and a second security question not constitute true multifactor authentication?
column4: Both are knowledge evidence; MFA requires evidence from different factor categories.
column5: A password plus a hardware token combines knowledge and possession. Counting two items from one category does not provide the cross-category protection MFA requires.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 26
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield Authentication MFA Factors Source::Messer-v140

column2: What does an emailed registration link or verification code demonstrate?
column4: That the user can access the specified email account.
column5: Email verification associates account activity with control of an address. It does not independently establish the person's real-world identity.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 26
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic Authentication Email Verification Source::Messer-v140

column2: An organization wants users to generate changing login codes on their phones without receiving a text message for every attempt. Which method fits?
column5: The application acts as a software token. It avoids dependence on per-login SMS delivery, although the phone and the application's secret still require protection.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 26
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield Authentication AuthenticatorApp MFA Source::Messer-v140

column2: What MFA factor does a dedicated hardware token primarily provide?
column5: The token may generate or hold authentication evidence. A PIN used with the token adds knowledge; a second carried token would still be another possession item.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 26
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic Authentication HardwareToken MFA Source::Messer-v140

column2: What security dependency do SMS-delivered codes and voice-call codes share?
column4: Both depend on control of the registered phone number and the security of the telephone channel.
column5: Messages or calls may be redirected, intercepted, or received after a number is reassigned. The two delivery methods are different, but they share that channel risk.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 26
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield Authentication SMS VoiceCall Risk Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which open standard allows one service to rely on authentication and authorization assertions supplied by a separate identity provider?
column5:
SAML carries assertions between trust participants. SSO describes the user's one-login experience; SAML is one standard that can help implement federated access.

Source Note

The official Objective 2.1 PDF prints “Security Assertions Markup Language.” The approved Messer notes use the standard singular expansion, “Security Assertion Markup Language,” which this card follows.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 27
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic Authentication SAML Federation Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which access method lets a user authenticate once and then use assigned resources without a new credential prompt for each resource during the session?
column5: SSO reduces repeated authentication prompts. It does not grant every possible permission; authorization still determines which resources are assigned.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 27
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield Authentication SSO Identity Source::Messer-v140

column2: An administrator receives elevated credentials only for one approved session, and they are removed afterward. Which control is this?
column5: Temporary elevation reduces the time during which a stolen everyday account could hold administrator rights. It applies least privilege to access duration.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 27
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield PrivilegedAccess JustInTime LeastPrivilege Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which broader security system vaults powerful credentials, controls requests for their use, and records privileged activity?
column5: PAM governs administrator and root access as a program. Just-in-time elevation is one PAM technique, not a synonym for the complete management system.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 27
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield PrivilegedAccess PAM Auditing Source::Messer-v140

column2: An organization must centrally require screen locks and control applications and company data on enrolled phones. Which control fits?
column5: MDM applies device policy from a central service. IAM governs identities and access more broadly; MDM focuses on the managed mobile endpoint.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 27
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield MobileSecurity MDM Policy Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which logical control identifies sensitive information and blocks or controls its unauthorized disclosure through endpoints, email, or cloud services?
column5: DLP protects the movement and disclosure of data. It is not a backup system and does not replace identity or device-access controls.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 27
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield DataSecurity DLP SensitiveData Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which security discipline manages digital identities, authentication, authorization, and access changes throughout an identity's lifecycle?
column5:
IAM aims to give the right identity the right access at the right time and to remove access when it is no longer justified. A directory can support IAM but is not the entire discipline.

Source Note

The official Objective 2.1 PDF prints “Identity access management.” The approved Messer notes use “Identity and Access Management,” which this card follows.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 27
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield Identity IAM AccessControl Source::Messer-v140

column2: Which service centralizes records for users, computers, groups, printers, and other network resources to support authentication and access administration?
column5: A directory is a structured source of identities and resources. IAM uses such information within the broader lifecycle and governance process.
column9: CompTIA 220-1202 Exam Objectives v4.0, Objective 2.1; Professor Messer 220-1202 v1.40, p. 27
column10: A+::220-1202::2.1 A+::220-1202::Domain2-Security A+::220-1202::SecurityMeasuresandPurposes Basic HighYield Identity DirectoryServices Centralization Source::Messer-v140
